narrative_blocks.header: Dance teacher, globe-trotter, and loyal MGH volunteer
narrative_blocks.body: Christine Ann Woleslagle worked as a flight attendant for Air New England — where she met her husband James Hooker Hamersley II, then a pilot — and ground operations for Swiss Air, then became a devoted figure in Boston's adult dance community at the Jeannette Neill Dance Studio, creating the Facebook page 'Dance Affinity' with daily postings. She served on the Ladies Visiting Committee of Massachusetts General Hospital, traveled to India, Vietnam, and the Canadian Rockies, and died on March 21, 2024 in Hanover, Massachusetts — buried at Quogue Cemetery beside her husband, two lives that had met in Air New England's cockpit and ended in New England.

burial.plot_notes: Quogue Cemetery Suffolk NY per FA

naming_inspiration.description: 'James Hooker' is a recurring name in the Hamersley family across four generations: his great-great-grandfather Judge James Hooker (H00797), his great-grandfather James Hooker Hamersley (H03408), and now James Hooker Hamersley II (H05209). The 'Hooker' middle name honors the ancestral descent from Rev. Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Hooker; Army Intelligence and aviation captain
narrative_blocks.body: James Hooker Hamersley II carried 'Hooker' as his middle name — as the Hamersley family had in the name 'James Hooker Hamersley' for three generations before him — and built a life at distance from the Gilded Age inheritance: he attended the Hun School and Washington and Lee University, served as a captain with Army Intelligence in the 101st Airborne Division, and became a captain with Air New England as a pilot, a writer, and a photographer. He married Christine Ann Woleslagle in April 1981 in Mount Lebanon, Pennsylvania, and they settled in Duxbury, Massachusetts, where he died on December 24, 1999; his wife Christine, a flight attendant and Boston dance community figure, died in March 2024 — the last of H04621's immediate family.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
